data.title: How AI model predicts heavy rainstorms 4 hours in advance
data.description:
Hong Kong and mainland Chinese scholars have developed a pioneering AI model that uses satellite data to forecast heavy rainstorms up to four hours before they strike, much earlier than current predictions allow.
The model represented a “major advancement in atmospheric monitoring and severe weather early warnings, enabling faster and more accurate forecasts and strengthening regional disaster preparedness and response”, Dr Dai Kuai, a postdoctoral fellow in the University of Science and Technology’s department of civil and environmental engineering, said on Wednesday.
Researchers from the university and mainland institutions, including national meteorological agencies, published a paper online last month on the “deep diffusion model of satellite data”. It is the world’s first weather forecasting artificial intelligence (AI) model using satellite data for thunderstorm “nowcasting” and predicting rainstorms up to four hours in advance.
Precise forecasts of small-scale, rapidly developing thunderstorms or rainstorms at present are typically limited to between 20 minutes and two hours in advance.
The model is trained with data from a Chinese satellite to capture the evolution of convective cloud structures. It can spot early signs of convection developments to predict rainfall and enable more timely severe weather warnings.

Atmospheric convection refers to the movement of air molecules and cloud particles caused by temperature differences. Strong upwards motion of air produces condensation, forming liquid or solid cloud particles.
Under strong convection, cloud particles grow into massive thunderhead clouds and will fall, and are therefore indicators of impending heavy rain. Convective weather phenomena include lightning, rainstorms, tornadoes and thunderstorms.
According to Dai, conventional weather forecasts, which rely mainly on ground-based radar, scan for signals “from the ground to above” and can therefore often detect changes “only after convective clouds have already formed”.
To overcome these challenges, Dai and his team developed a new AI model and trained it with infrared brightness temperature data collected by China’s FengYun-4A satellite from 2018 to 2021 to accurately capture convective cloud structures.
By harnessing satellite data that monitors cloud evolution from space, the new AI model can visualise and identify signs of rising air development much earlier.
The forecasts are updated about every 15 minutes and cover broad regions including China, South Korea and Southeast Asia. The model may also be useful for predicting heavy rainstorms in regions lacking radar or automatic weather station coverage.


Su Hui, chair professor in the university’s civil and environmental engineering department, said satellite data might not be able to capture highly localised rainstorms affecting only small parts of Hong Kong, as the convection could be too small to show up in the satellite imagery.
But the model could be useful for predicting vigorous upward movements of air ahead of extreme events such as Super Typhoon Ragasa last year, Su said.
Hong Kong last year issued five black rainstorm warnings and experienced 14 tropical cyclones, the highest number since 1946. Ragasa struck Hong Kong in September, injuring about 100 people. It was the second time in 2025 that the highest typhoon signal was issued.
Su said the team was in talks with the Hong Kong Observatory on implementing the technology to boost weather forecasting in the city.
A spokesman for the Observatory said it would maintain collaborations with academia with a view to enhancing weather forecasting capability.
This week, tech giant Nvidia released a study on a model that uses satellite data with ground-based radar data to predict the evolution of cloud and rainfall, offering forecasts up to six hours in advance. The university’s paper was among those cited as a reference.

data.title: Hong Kong eyes lowering age bar for health checks on commercial drivers to 65
data.description:
Hong Kong plans to lower the age threshold for mandatory medical certification for commercial vehicle drivers from 70 to 65 under legislative proposals to be submitted in the first quarter of this year, with the new rules supported by a transport advisory body.
The government on Wednesday briefed the Transport Advisory Committee on the proposals to tighten medical fitness requirements for commercial vehicle drivers in a bid to further safeguard road safety amid an ageing workforce in the sector.
Under the proposed changes, the age threshold for commercial vehicle drivers to submit medical examination certificates will be lowered from 70 to 65.
Additionally, the validity of their driving licences will be reduced to one year, meaning that an annual check-up is required before they can be renewed.
Authorities also proposed enhancing physical fitness requirements by updating the list of diseases and physical disabilities that prohibit driving, with updated vision standards and additional requirements applicable to the drivers.
Another proposal would empower the commissioner for transport to require reports from any driver with dubious health conditions. Failure to comply could lead to suspension or cancellation of a driving licence.
The government will brief the Legislative Council’s transport panel on the results of a trial it conducted earlier and the legislative proposals within the first quarter of this year.
The subsidiary legislation amendments will be submitted to the Legco to implement the new arrangements as soon as possible following their completion.
Professor Wong Sze-chun, chairman of the Transport Advisory Committee – an advisory body to the government on transport policy and proposals – said its members supported the legislative amendments.
“Members have agreed that a more robust assessment mechanism is essential for enhancing road safety, and the more frequent check-ups will raise commercial vehicle drivers’ awareness of their own physical conditions and facilitate early treatment for those with remediable conditions,” he said.
“These can extend the safe driving years of aged drivers and support the sustainable operation of the industry.
“Members fully support the government in moving forward with the legislative amendments and stepping up publicity to ensure the industry is well prepared for the new requirements.”

Authorities reviewed the requirements and mechanism for medical certification of driving licence applicants and holders under the Road Traffic (Driving Licences) Regulations, with the goal of improving road safety.
The Transport and Logistics Bureau earlier invited members of the commercial vehicle industry to join a medical assessment trial in the fourth quarter of last year.
The bureau earlier said in a document to the Legco that it would continue carrying out preparatory work, such as producing demonstration videos for frontline medical practitioners and publishing medical guidelines, striving for the implementation of the new arrangements in the second quarter of this year.
The move followed a series of serious accidents involving elderly cabbies that sparked public concern.
Last August, an 80-year-old cab driver crashed into a wall at the Nina Hotel in Tsuen Wan, killing a 35-year-old tourist from the Philippines. The driver reportedly said he felt dizzy and lost control of the vehicle.
The number of accidents involving elderly taxi drivers has surged in recent years, with those aged 70 or above involved in 651 accidents in 2024, compared with 626 in 2023 and 454 in 2022.

data.title: Hygon’s chips ‘safe’ from AMD security flaw amid China’s tech self-reliance push
data.description:
Chips from Chinese semiconductor designer Hygon Information Technology were found unaffected by a recently disclosed vulnerability in products developed by its US tech licenser and former joint venture partner Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), according to analysts, which showed progress in China’s self-reliance drive.
Hygon’s central processing units (CPUs), which were developed under a licence to use AMD’s X86-based Zen chip architecture, did not have the hardware security flaw that affected a range of AMD artificial intelligence chips that were widely installed in various data centres and cloud infrastructure operations, according to Chen Bingxin, an analyst at semiconductors consultancy JW Insights.
Chen attributed that result to Hygon’s years of investments in redesigning AMD’s chip architecture with self-developed innovations.
It underscored efforts by Chinese chip designers to become less exposed to Western technology risks.
AMD and Hygon did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Wednesday.
Hygon’s successful initiative to diverge from AMD’s original chip architecture marked a milestone in China, where developers are pushing for increased autonomy in core technology and security for locally designed semiconductors, according to JW Insights’ Chen.

The hardware security flaw affecting AMD’s chips, code-named StackWarp, was disclosed in a report published on January 15, by the German cybersecurity institution CISPA Helmholtz Centre for Information Security.
A team of researchers at the centre said the vulnerability, found in AMD’s flagship Zen 1 through Zen 5 processor families, could be exploited to bypass key cloud security features and “posed a serious risk to cloud services”.
The vulnerability targeted AMD’s SEV-SNP technology, which was designed to protect its chips during sensitive cloud computing workloads, according to the researchers.
Hygon, meanwhile, used its self-developed CSV3 technology on its chips for the same purpose, which made its products immune to attackers looking to exploit the StackWarp flaw, according to JW Insights’ Chen.
He said the impact of AMD’s hardware security flaw showed further bifurcation in x86 chip architecture development, as Hygon showed that it had established a fully self-reliant x86 ecosystem in China.
X86 was originally a family of complex computer architectures developed by US semiconductor giant Intel. Most server, desktop and laptop computers are now based on various iterations of that architecture.
AMD has already released a software patch to counter the StackWarp vulnerability, but this workaround was expected to cause a loss in performance for the affected chips. That created a dilemma for cloud services operators, as they weighed security against performance amid rising AI demand.
Founded in 2014, Hygon, which designs CPUs and accelerators for AI applications, is a major player in China’s drive for tech self-sufficiency. Its chips were widely used in the cloud computing, finance, energy and government sectors, according to its website.

data.title: Transport chief responds to backlash over Hong Kong law on public bus seat belts
data.description:
Hong Kong’s transport minister has promised to refine a policy requiring seat belts to be worn on buses by ensuring the devices become more user-friendly, while acknowledging lawmakers’ calls for a review of the recent law.
Secretary for Transport and Logistics Mable Chan made the pledge on Wednesday, less than a week after the new rules took effect that require all passengers travelling on public or private buses, including franchised and school services, to wear seat belts where available.
The policy has already prompted public backlash and calls for a review within six to nine months from the city’s major political parties. Professor Wong Sze-chun, chairman of the Transport Advisory Committee, later said a review was “not that important” as such legislation often needed an adjustment period.
Chan said she had heard lawmakers’ comments, but stopped short of saying how she planned to respond to calls for a review or for amendments. She also refrained from addressing the safety risks facing standing passengers not required to buckle up.
“We have also heard suggestions from lawmakers. Some hoped we would review the rule after a period of time to make improvements and optimisations,” she said. “These are valuable opinions and feedback.
“Whether [the seat belts] are too tight or too loose, we are willing to follow up on and improve as quickly as possible these operational and equipment issues.”
She responded to calls for buses on short-haul routes to be exempt from the rule, saying some of the services passed through highways.
The minister also promised to gather public feedback on the policy, reiterating that enforcement efforts would initially focus on educating and persuading passengers.
The law, which took effect on January 25, also applies to the rear seats of private light buses and goods vehicles, as well as to all seats in special-purpose vehicles such as mobile cranes or street-cleaning trucks, provided they were first registered on or after that day.
But some passengers have complained about the inconveniences of buckling up and criticised the seat belts as too cumbersome for children. Other commuters are opting to stand while travelling on buses.
Anyone convicted of breaching the law faces a maximum fine of HK$5,000 (US$641) and up to three months in prison.
In a related development, the KMB reported to police on Wednesday that two seat belts were cut on a double-decker for the 86K route running between Kam Ying Court and Sha Tin MTR station. The force has classified the case as criminal damage.

On the legislative front, the Hong Kong Federation of Trade Unions, the Liberal Party, the New People’s Party and the Democratic Alliance for the Betterment and Progress of Hong Kong (DAB) – the city’s largest political party – are among those calling for the law to be reviewed in up to nine months.
Lawmaker Lam Wai-kong, who is also chairman of the Motor Transport Workers General Union, urged authorities to reassess the legislation after a transition period, particularly regarding the severity of the punishment.
The legislator said the government could have conducted a more thorough consultation with the industry to address concerns.
He noted that authorities had not adopted some of the union’s key recommendations, including detailed guidelines on drivers’ responsibilities and how the rules apply in different circumstances for public education.
The DAB’s Ben Chan Han-pan, who sat on a Legco committee that scrutinised the bill last September, was among the first to call for reassessing the law.
“At the time, we discussed it purely from a safety perspective,” he said. “Would there be such a backlash after implementation? Perhaps we did not assess it in sufficient detail back then.”
Liberal Party chairman Peter Shiu Ka-fai said the rule was well-intentioned despite the backlash. He also voiced support for a review of the penalties and suggested studying whether distinctions should be made between short and long-haul journeys.
In a 2007 policy paper, the Transport Department said that enforcing compulsory seat belt use on urban buses or those allowing standing passengers would be difficult, citing enforcement challenges such as passengers being able to stand or move around inside vehicles.
Eleven years later, an independent review committee for franchised bus services was set up following a bus crash on Tai Po Road that left 19 dead and 66 injured.
The committee commissioned two transport experts from Australia and the United Kingdom to share opinions on the implementation of a seat belt requirement for public buses.

The pair included Professor John Stanley of the University of Sydney’s Institute of Transport and Logistics Studies, who stressed the importance of conducting a cost-benefit analysis concerning the feasibility of installing seat belts in pre-existing buses and the reduction in accident rates.
He also recommended holding a consultation involving bus manufacturers and police, as well as seeking professional advice from institutions’ research centres specialising in accident studies.
The report also reviewed seat belt regulations in other jurisdictions, such as the United States, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands, Singapore, Canada, New Zealand and the Australian state of Victoria.
None of the jurisdictions above mandate seat belts on buses designed for urban use, the report found.
According to the report, the US, UK and the Netherlands regulate the installation of seat belts in buses operating intercity or cross-border routes.
Singapore does not mandate seat belts on public buses but does so for smaller private-hire buses.
Following the experts’ suggestions, the Transport Department tasked an independent consultancy in 2019 to conduct a cost-benefit analysis on the installation of seat belts on the upper decks of public buses.
Additional reporting by Fiona Sun and Danny Mok

data.title: Muyuan Foods courts cornerstone investors for Hong Kong listing
data.description:
Thailand’s Charoen Pokphand Group plans to invest in the Hong Kong listing of Chinese pig breeder Muyuan Foods, people familiar with the matter said, in what is shaping up to be one of the city’s largest share sales this year.
CP Group was poised to participate as a cornerstone investor – which typically receives a guaranteed allocation in exchange for agreeing to hold shares for a set period – in the listing, the people said, asking not to be identified to discuss a private matter.
Other potential cornerstone investors included investment firm Fidelity International and UBS Group’s asset management arm, the people added.
Muyuan, one of the world’s biggest pig breeders and pork producers, was set to start taking investor orders as early as Thursday for a listing that could raise about US$1.5 billion, the people said.
Deliberations are ongoing, and details of the deal may still change. Representatives for Muyuan, CP Group, Fidelity and UBS declined to comment.
The Henan-based firm is among a crop of Chinese companies in the consumer industry that are defying China’s sluggish consumer demand to sell shares in Hong Kong. Energy-drink maker Eastroc Beverage Group is close to debuting in the city, while snack retailer Busy Ming Group saw a first-day pop.
Hong Kong is also set to close the month with record proceeds raised for any January from share listings.
The city currently has more than 350 companies waiting to sell shares, Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing CEO Bonnie Chan said in an interview last week.
Muyuan traces its roots to a pig farm established in 1995. It recently reported preliminary net income of 14.7 billion yuan (US$2.1 billion) to 15.7 billion yuan for 2025.

data.title: Hong Kong logs US$37 million Admiralty office deal amid market recovery hopes
data.description:
A Hong Kong office unit at Admiralty Centre has changed hands for HK$292.17 million (US$37.46 million), making it the most expensive commercial property transaction registered on Tuesday, according to official records, in a deal that adds to tentative signs of stabilisation in the city’s battered real estate market.
The office space, located in Tower 1 of the Admiralty Centre within the city’s core business district, was acquired by Luck Ring Development, Land Registry records showed.
The unit measures 21,318 sq ft, according to agents, putting the price at about HK$13,705 per square foot.
Luck Ring’s sole shareholder is Wah Mei Co, a firm registered in the British Virgin Islands, according to Companies Registry filings.
Its three directors hold roles across a range of Hong Kong businesses, including Daniel & Co, a retailer of Japanese toys and collectibles such as Sanrio’s Hello Kitty, Bandai’s Tamagotchi and products from the Dragon Ball Super series.
The group did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Government records show the office was previously leased to tenants including the Philippine Overseas Labor Office and China National Geological & Mining (HK), which paid monthly rents ranging from HK$228,000 to HK$253,000 in 2014 and 2018 respectively.
The transaction was among a handful of sizeable office deals completed in recent months, as property agents pointed to improving sentiment in the commercial segment.
In December, US-based video technology firm Vobile Group bought the top floor of the Bank of America Tower in Admiralty for HK$198 million, according to official records.
The company, which helps major Hollywood studios protect and monetise online video content and listed in Hong Kong in 2018, acquired the 37th floor of the grade A building. The unit spans about 10,500 sq ft and includes two private sky gardens totalling roughly 3,076 sq ft.
Renewed investor interest has coincided with a pickup in leasing activity.
Hong Kong’s grade A office market recorded positive net absorption of 537,000 sq ft in December, marking a ninth consecutive month of expansion, according to JLL.
“This sustained momentum is a clear sign of a gradual recovery in Hong Kong’s office market,” said Alex Barnes, managing director of JLL in Hong Kong, Macau and Taiwan.
“Leasing activity remains steady, and inquiry levels in January have stayed robust,” he said. “We expect other premium buildings in the district to benefit from spillover demand in the second half of the year, as a number of prime towers are now close to full occupancy.”

data.title: Moonshot’s newest release narrows US-China AI model development gap: analysts
data.description:
Moonshot AI’s newest release, Kimi K2.5, has narrowed the gap between the US and China to the closest it has ever been in the development of artificial intelligence models, according to third-party evaluations.
That feat by the Beijing-based start-up, founded in March 2023, has raised questions about the efficacy of US policies – most notably, export controls on advanced semiconductors – to constrain China’s AI development efforts, said Kyle Chan, a fellow of the John L Thornton China Centre at the Washington-based think tank Brookings Institution.
“It seems that, for these Chinese start-ups, it was just a matter of getting access to capital,” Chan said. “I wouldn’t have guessed that Chinese AI companies would continue to keep pace with their US peers as recently as a month or two ago.”
Moonshot was valued at US$4.3 billion after raising US$500 million in its latest Series C funding round in December, according to venture capital tracker ITJuzi. Investors include IDG Capital, Tencent Holdings and Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the South China Morning Post.
Released on Tuesday, Kimi K2.5 scored just a handful of points behind the leading models of US heavyweights OpenAI, Anthropic and Google DeepMind in comprehensive performance evaluations by Artificial Analysis, the third-party benchmarking firm said on Wednesday.
Like other Chinese AI firms, Moonshot publicly released the weights of its new model, meaning that developers around the world with the requisite hardware could freely download and customise the powerful 595-gigabyte model without paying for expensive subscriptions.
The open-source nature of such a powerful model challenges the strategy of American AI companies that develop closed-source models, according to Chan.
“Especially when it comes to subscription-based start-ups like OpenAI and Anthropic, when you have a Chinese model that is basically just as good and open source, it seems to be a real challenge to their business models,” he said.

Raw intelligence aside, Kimi K2.5 was also much cheaper to run than its US counterparts. Artificial Analysis estimated that Kimi K2.5 was more than four times cheaper than the leading models of OpenAI and Anthropic.
According to Moonshot, Kimi K2.5 was built on the same Mixture-of-Experts architecture that has helped drive efficiency gains for other Chinese AI companies over the past year. US restrictions on access to computational resources have forced these companies to maximise the cost efficiency of their models.
Moonshot’s new release marked its first multimodal model, which means it can process image and video in addition to text. That removed a “critical barrier” to the adoption of open models, as other leading Chinese models have yet to add this feature, according to Artificial Analysis.
A notable feature of Kimi K2.5 is “agent swarm”, which enables users to better tackle complex tasks by using up to 100 “subagents” that execute workflows in parallel.
Such parallel execution capabilities are widely seen as crucial for capturing software engineer users – a lucrative market for AI services.
However, the use of many subagents also increased the computational costs. Chinese company Zhipu AI, for example, last week decided to restrict parallel usage of its flagship coding product owing to computational constraints.
Moonshot, meanwhile, has restricted use of its new model’s agent swarm feature to premium users because of similar concerns about available computing power, according to a source familiar with the matter.
That suggested how US export controls have had an effect on China’s AI development efforts, even if these have not hurt the performance of individual Chinese models, as the country successfully pursued a “fast follower” approach, according to Lennart Heim, an AI policy and semiconductor expert.

data.title: Beijing clears Nvidia’s H200 imports, easing chip crunch for China tech giants
data.description:
Beijing has begun approving imports of Nvidia’s H200 graphics processing units (GPUs), according to two sources familiar with the matter, ending regulatory uncertainty over the US tech giant’s second most powerful artificial intelligence chip.
The first batch was expected to go to Big Tech companies, which were in urgent need of the GPU, a source said. However, access for state-backed companies, such as telecommunication network operators, was expected to remain under tight control, the source added.
The approval covered more than 400,000 units for ByteDance, Alibaba Group Holding and Tencent Holdings, while other firms awaited further approvals, according to Reuters, which first reported the news on Wednesday. Alibaba owns the Post.
Nvidia did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The move follows Beijing’s temporary hold on H200 shipments earlier this month after Washington cleared exports. It reflects Beijing’s effort to balance between demand for top-performance AI chips and its quest for tech self-sufficiency.
Imports of the H200 reflected a “balanced outcome”, said Randy Abrams, head of Taiwan research at UBS Group. Unlimited access to Nvidia’s advanced chips could hinder the growth of China’s domestic industry, making some form of framework necessary for regulators to limit adoption percentage and manage demand, he added.
“Beijing’s approval of the H200 is driven by purely strategic motives,” said Alex Capri, a senior lecturer at National University of Singapore’s business school. “Ultimately, this decision is taken to further China’s indigenous capabilities and, by extension, the competitive capabilities of China tech.”

The GPU, cleared by Washington earlier this month on condition that the shipments do not exceed 50 per cent of the volume sold domestically in the US, remains a key option for Chinese tech giants to train their AI models.
China’s “extreme hunger for high-quality computing power” had temporarily overtaken the goal of tech self-reliance, said Arisa Liu, chief director and research fellow at Taiwan Industry Economics Services, a databank of the Taiwan Institute of Economic Research.
The GPU would have massive market potential in China, with its demand likely to extend beyond internet firms to sectors such as autonomous driving and finance, Liu added.
During the temporary ban, some Chinese AI companies considered sourcing H200 chips from the black market at much higher prices or settling for lower performance domestic alternatives from the likes of Huawei Technologies, the Post reported last week.
One reseller, who said he received “a couple of inquiries” last week, said a bundled server with eight H200 GPUs was selling for about 2.3 million yuan (US$330,400), roughly 50 per cent above the official list price in mainland China.
The approval also coincides with Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang’s visit to China, underscoring the company’s commitment to the market.
Huang, 62, began his latest trip to China last week, touring Shanghai, Beijing and Shenzhen, after attending the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. He received a rock-star welcome at Nvidia’s Lunar New Year celebrations in Shanghai and Beijing. He met Shanghai staff to review milestones and product pipelines, Tencent’s news portal reported on Friday. He did not mention the H200 at those meetings, according to the report.

data.title: Hong Kong police arrest 7 over bogus HK$10 million bankruptcy loan scheme
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested seven members of a fraud syndicate accused of swindling nearly 100 victims out of HK$10 million (US$1.3 million) by luring them into taking out loans under a bogus bankruptcy scheme and then charging hefty handling fees that left them even further out of pocket.
The case first surfaced last year after four victims reported receiving cold calls offering help in securing low-interest loans. They were later invited to an office in Kwai Fong, which falsely claimed to be an accounting firm, where staff promoted a “personal mini-bankruptcy plan”, according to the regional anti-deception unit of New Territories South.
Victims were told that by joining the scheme, they would not need to repay existing debts and would avoid normal bankruptcy restrictions, such as limits on buying property, vehicles or applying for new loans.
Once victims expressed interest, they were taken to a finance company in Tsim Sha Tsui and persuaded to borrow sums ranging from HK$38,000 to HK$170,000, at annual interest rates as high as 47 per cent.
After receiving the loan funds, staff immediately took the money under the pretext of referral handling fees. Victims were then directed to a designated law firm to process the supposed bankruptcy plan, only to discover that they were actually applying for formal bankruptcy.
When the victims realised that the “mini-bankruptcy” scheme did not exist, the fraudsters could no longer be contacted.
An investigation identified a local crime syndicate operating out of three locations: the Kwai Fong office posing as an accounting firm, the Tsim Sha Tsui finance company, and a call centre in the same district.

On Monday, officers intercepted a man at the finance company after he had collected HK$70,000 from a potential victim. Simultaneous raids in Kwai Fong and Tsim Sha Tsui led to six further arrests.
In total, five men and two women aged between 28 and 38 were detained on suspicion of conspiracy to defraud. Officers seized 17 computers, 20 mobile phones, about HK$1.77 million in cash, as well as luxury watches and handbags believed to be the proceeds of crime.
The suspects remain in custody and police said further arrests had not been ruled out.
Police reminded the public to be wary of unsolicited calls offering loans, debt restructuring or bankruptcy services. They stressed that there is no such thing as a “personal mini-bankruptcy plan” in Hong Kong.
In the city, formal Individual Voluntary Arrangement (IVA) or bankruptcy procedures must be handled through licensed lawyers or accountants and ultimately approved by the court. Any claims suggesting otherwise are false and misleading.

data.title: ‘Teach our children well’: Jack Ma urges changes to China’s education in AI era
data.description:
Education in China’s countryside should shift away from rote-based learning to focus on nurturing curiosity and creativity in the artificial intelligence era, Alibaba Group Holding founder Jack Ma said in a meeting with rural teachers on Monday.
In remarks published by the Jack Ma Foundation on Tuesday, former English teacher Ma said the dawn of the AI era posed a challenge for rural education, but also presented an opportunity to “return to the fundamentals of education”. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
“In the AI era, the question is no longer whether to use AI, but how to teach our children to use AI well,” Ma told teachers in a video conference call.
“Education should no longer be about making children compete with AI in calculation and memory. Instead, we should help children maintain their curiosity, as curiosity is the source of ‘computing power’.”
That assessment reiterated Ma’s message to rural teachers last year, when he said AI would reshape the world over the next decade.
Since 2015, Ma has met annually with 100 new recruits to his foundation’s Rural Teachers Initiative, which identifies promising rural teachers around the country and provides them with financial support and professional training.
His latest comments on AI’s impact on rural education came in response to concerns expressed by this year’s recruits that the rural education system would be “left behind” in the AI era, according to the foundation.


“The real gap in the AI era is not a technological gap, but rather a gap in curiosity, imagination, creativity, judgment and collaboration,” Ma was quoted by the foundation as saying.
“In the AI era, our education system must not aim to have a thousand students give the same correct answer, but rather to teach a thousand students to ask ten thousand different, good questions.”
Ma’s comments marked the third time in the past year that he acknowledged the transformative power of AI.
In his last public remarks at an Alibaba Cloud event in April, Ma said the responsibility of technologists was not to develop AI systems that replaced humans, but rather to make AI better understand and serve humanity. Alibaba Cloud is the AI and cloud computing services arm of Hangzhou-based Alibaba.
Both the Alibaba Foundation and flagship consumer app Qwen were listed as supporters of the Rural Teachers Initiative.
Earlier this month, the Jack Ma Foundation and Qwen donated AI devices, including Alibaba’s new Quark AI glasses, to a rural school in Yibin – a city in southwestern Sichuan province – to promote “AI familiarity” among students, the school said in an announcement.
In recent years, Ma has maintained a low profile since stepping down from all his corporate positions at Alibaba, although he remains the company’s single largest shareholder.
His latest comments come as other Chinese AI firms have also sounded the alarm on AI’s potential impact on society. On Tuesday, Xinhua News Agency reported that government authorities were preparing a dedicated policy document to address the likely impact of AI on employment.
In November, DeepSeek senior researcher Chen Deli warned that AI developers were working towards completely replacing human labour, despite the potentially destabilising effects of such advancement.

data.title: Price rises on horizon for Chinese cloud firms after Google, AWS set new rates
data.description:
Several Chinese cloud service providers are “evaluating price increases”, according to analysts, following recent moves by Google and Amazon Web Services (AWS), as the explosive growth of artificial intelligence drives demand.
Google on Tuesday announced price rises of up to 100 per cent for certain services, effective May 1. For example, North American users of the CDN Interconnect, Direct Peering, and Carrier Peering services will pay US$0.08 per gigabyte, double the current rate. In Asia, the charge will be US$0.085 per gigabyte, up 42 per cent.
Similarly, AWS earlier this month raised the price of its “EC2 Capacity Blocks for ML” service by about 15 per cent across all regions. The service reserves accelerated compute instances to run machine learning workloads.
“The price increase stems from dual pressures of rising costs and surging demand,” said Liu Lihui, research director at market research firm IDC China. “On one hand, higher prices for upstream servers, storage, and memory, coupled with escalating electricity and energy costs, have driven up computing service expenses. On the other, corporate demand for AI computing power continues to grow rapidly.”
The increases “have broken the long-standing convention that prices [of cloud services] only go down and never up”, according to a research note by Everbright Securities.

IDC’s Liu added that “several Chinese cloud service providers are also evaluating and following up on this round of price increases, with a greater emphasis on assessing the impact of upstream hardware supply chains in their considerations”.
Everbright Securities analysts, however, projected China’s cloud services industry “to follow suit in slowing price cuts” for now.
Alibaba Cloud led China’s AI cloud services sector in the first half of 2025 with a 36 per cent market share. ByteDance’s Volcano Engine, Huawei Cloud and Tencent Cloud followed with 15, 13 and 7 per cent, respectively, according to market intelligence Omdia.
None of the four companies immediately responded to requests for comment. Alibaba Cloud is the AI and cloud computing unit of Alibaba Group Holding, which owns the Post.
Global tech giants are investing heavily in AI infrastructure. On Tuesday, Facebook owner Meta Platforms struck a deal worth up to US$6 billion with Corning to expand data centres in the US to support the company’s “apps, technologies, and AI ambitions”. A day earlier, Nvidia invested an extra US$2 billion in AI cloud builder CoreWeave as the two expanded the partnership to build more than 5 gigawatts of AI factories by 2030.
AI demand can be measured by tokens, the smallest units of data that are processed by models. Li Feifei, senior vice-president of Alibaba Cloud Intelligence, said last week that token consumption worldwide could grow by at least 100-fold, or even 1,000-fold, this year, while memory prices, which rose at least 30 per cent over the past few months, would climb by at least 200 per cent.
The cloud industry has long been locked in a price war to lure customers. Alibaba Cloud cut prices six times in 2014 and 17 times in the year ending October 2016. In 2024, the cloud units of Alibaba, Tencent Holdings, JD.com and China Mobile all reduced prices.
Additional reporting by Wency Chen and Iris Deng

data.title: Vanuatu seeks Hong Kong’s regulatory expertise, woos investors and tourists
data.description:
Vanuatu’s leader hopes to strengthen his country’s presence in Hong Kong as the Pacific island nation looks to tap the city’s regulatory expertise, financial markets and investment opportunities.
Prime Minister Jotham Napat said on Wednesday that his country had established a “communication mechanism” during his trip to Hong Kong, to improve coordination and deepen ties with the financial hub.
He called the visit a “pivotal moment” for bilateral relations, with his delegation holding high-level talks with senior Hong Kong officials, including Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu.
“It is encouraging to report that we reached a strong consensus on deepening our political mutual trust and institutional cooperation,” he said. “One of the key outcomes of our discussions is the establishment of a more stable and regular communication mechanism.”
He said the mechanism would improve coordination on regional affairs and help strengthen collaboration on development and economic issues.
“Our visit has solidified the long-term cooperation foundation between Vanuatu and Hong Kong in areas of mutual interest, such as traditional financial cooperation and trade,” Napat said.

The Pacific island nation of about 330,000 people has been seeking closer ties with Hong Kong to support its ambitions in digital finance and bolster cross-border trade cooperation.
An archipelago of 83 islands, Vanuatu is a prominent offshore financial hub and tax haven.
Last July, it hosted a conference in Hong Kong, where the country’s deputy prime minister called the city a “role model” and said Vanuatu had entered a “new phase of economic stability”.
The country also strengthened ties with Beijing by signing a Framework Agreement on Enhancing Economic Partnership last November.


Speaking to the Post afterwards, on the sidelines of Wednesday’s event, Napat said Vanuatu could learn from Hong Kong in many areas, such as the city’s regulatory expertise in finance.
He noted Vanuatu was looking to Hong Kong’s government and companies for help in developing a digital currency.
Vanuatu was also hoping to boost cooperation and investment from Hong Kong in areas such as tourism and agriculture, he said.
The prime minister added that he hoped having a presence in Hong Kong would “elevate” Vanuatu’s profile to attract investors and tourists from the region to his country.
In a statement issued a day earlier, Lee said the “prospects for cooperation between the two places are broad and promising” and that both jurisdictions should “seize opportunities presented by the Belt and Road Initiative”.
The initiative refers to Beijing’s scheme to link dozens of economies into a China-centred trade network.

data.title: Hong Kong’s Exchange Fund reports record earnings in 2025
data.description:
The Exchange Fund posted its best annual gain since the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) was set up in 1993, as solid stock market returns and rising gold prices boosted the city’s financial war chest used to defend the local currency.
The Exchange Fund’s full-year earnings for 2025 jumped 51 per cent to HK$331 billion (US$42 billion), compared with HK$218.8 billion in 2024, the HKMA said on Wednesday.
The returns surpassed the previous record of HK$264 billion set in 2017.
“Overall, the global financial markets showed strong resilience in 2025,” said Eddie Yue Wai-man, chief executive of the HKMA, at a media briefing. “Major stock markets saw broad-based gains, with many of them setting record highs.”
The price of gold, which rose 64 per cent last year, also benefited the Exchange Fund. The fund has some holdings of the precious metal, but it was not substantial, Yue said.
“Gold is not a liquid asset, so the Exchange Fund does not invest heavily in it. The Exchange Fund maintains a diversified portfolio and does not chase a particular asset,” Yue said.

He added that it was rare for all the main components of the Exchange Fund to record positive returns in a single year, noting that it had happened only twice before – in 2017 and 2020.
Looking ahead, Yue said there was a lot of uncertainty over the Federal Reserve’s interest rate trajectory and its impact on equity investments.
The stellar returns bolstered the fund’s contributions to the government’s fiscal reserves to HK$16.5 billion in 2025, compared with HK$13.2 billion a year earlier.
They also pushed the fund’s total assets to HK$4.151 trillion at the end of December, compared with HK$4.082 trillion in 2024.
The rate of return for the fund last year stood at 8 per cent, compared with a compounded annual return of 4.6 per cent from 1994 to 2025.
The Exchange Fund’s fourth-quarter earnings stood at HK$46.2 billion, a turnaround from a loss of HK$20.3 billion in the fourth quarter of 2024. The fund recorded positive returns in all four quarters last year.
The fund, which traces its formation to 1935, provides backing to the issuance of banknotes in the city and defends the Hong Kong dollar from attacks by speculators and currency short-sellers. The city’s de facto central bank invests the funds in Hong Kong equities and overseas stocks, global bonds, real estate and other long-term projects.
For the full year, the fund’s Hong Kong equity investments gained HK$33.9 billion, a 56 per cent increase from HK$21.8 billion a year earlier.
“The Exchange Fund can continue to perform well in 2026 amid a low interest rate environment, but it may not repeat the record high performance last year,” said Kenny Tang Sing-hing, chairman of the Hong Kong Institute of Financial Analysts and Professional Commentators.
The city’s benchmark Hang Seng Index jumped 28 per cent in 2025, adding to an 18 per cent gain in 2024. The rally was fuelled by Chinese technology stocks, particularly after artificial intelligence start-up DeepSeek’s technological breakthrough in January last year, when it introduced low-cost, high-efficiency AI models.
The Exchange Fund’s overseas stocks earned HK$74.1 billion, 8 per cent higher than HK$69.1 billion in 2024.
That echoes the performance of the S&P 500 Index, which rose 17 per cent in 2025 after a gain of 23 per cent in 2024.
Bond investments gained HK$142.2 billion in 2025, 4.6 per cent higher than HK$135.9 billion in 2024.
The foreign-exchange appreciation on non-US dollar assets brought a gain of HK$38.4 billion, a turnaround from a loss of HK$35.6 billion in 2024.
The change in the valuation of the Exchange Fund’s investments in overseas property and other long-term projects in the first nine months of 2025 led to a gain of HK$42.4 billion, compared with HK$28.5 billion a year earlier.
The HKMA typically reports the full-year performance of its long-term investments after a gap of a few months.
“The global stock market sentiment remains strong currently, which would benefit the Exchange Fund,” said Kenny Ng Lai-yin, a strategist at Everbright Securities International.
“The bond market may be more volatile this year as the market widely expects the US to cut rates further, which may affect returns. Overall, the Exchange Fund is likely to continue to deliver stable returns.”

data.title: Kai Tak Arena to host League of Legends Champions Korea Cup esports event
data.description:
Some of the biggest names in esports will descend on Hong Kong next month for the finals of the League of Legends Champions Korea (LCK) Cup.
Following on from the successful Blast Premier event at Cyberport last November, the LCK Cup represents another step up for gaming in the city.
Kai Tak Arena will host the tournament, from February 28 to March 1, the first time the event has been held outside South Korea.
Chow Kai-hong, chairman of the Hong Kong Esports Association, said they had received numerous requests for esports competitions to be held in the city, and believed more would come in the future.

“I hope there can be different large-scale tournaments to improve the atmosphere in Hong Kong, gradually igniting the industry,” he said. “Personally, I also hope to see if there’s an opportunity for Hong Kong to create its own local league.”
The LCK Cup Finals have reportedly been given M Mark status, and Aiden Lee, the competition’s general secretary, said taking it to Hong Kong could prove to be “a groundbreaking milestone for the global esports industry”.
M Mark status is awarded to recognised major sports events held in Hong Kong.

data.title: Pop Mart rallies in Hong Kong on aggressive push into US retail market
data.description:
Pop Mart extended its rally in Hong Kong on Wednesday after unveiling an accelerated push into the US market, underscoring how overseas expansion is helping the Chinese toymaker offset lingering pressures at home.
The maker of Labubu figurines plans to open more than 20 new stores across the United States this year, under a partnership with US mall operator Simon Property Group, which announced the roll-out in mid-January.
The outlets will be located in major shopping centres including King of Prussia in Philadelphia, Sawgrass Mills in Florida and The Westchester in New York, marking a step-change in Pop Mart’s overseas ambitions.
Shares of Pop Mart climbed as much as 9 per cent to HK$236 in afternoon trading, before ending 7 per cent higher at HK$231.4, extending a rebound fuelled by a HK$350 million (US$45 million) share buy-back last week. The stock has now risen more than 32 per cent from its year-to-date low of about HK$174.
The overseas momentum comes after a turbulent period in mainland China, where Pop Mart faced heightened scrutiny over its blind-box sales model.
Blind-box sales involve selling sealed products with undisclosed contents, a format that raised regulatory concerns over compulsive spending and gambling-like behaviour, particularly among minors.
Prices of its flagship Labubu series also slumped in the secondary market last year, hit by oversupply and fading hype, dragging the company’s shares lower in the second half.

Meanwhile, Pop Mart has been stepping up its push abroad, particularly in the Americas.
In the first half of 2025, it added a net 19 stores in the region, compared with 12 in China and four in Europe. By the end of June, the company operated 41 outlets across the Americas, according to its interim report in September.
That expansion has translated into explosive growth. Revenue from the Americas surged 1,142 per cent year on year to 2.26 billion yuan (US$315 million) in the first six months of last year, far outpacing the 135 per cent growth recorded in China.
The strategy also aligns with Beijing’s broader push for mainland companies to seek growth overseas, rather than intensifying competition at home amid deflationary pressures.
“With greater emphasis on intellectual property development, Chinese brands can increase recognition and gain a stronger foothold in consumer markets such as pop culture and sportswear,” said Gary Ng Cheuk-yan, a senior economist at Natixis Corporate and Investment Bank.
“Pop Mart’s move will not make it easier for others to enter the US market, but it reflects that the consumer sector is relatively less politically sensitive.”
Investment banks have taken note of the company’s resilience.
Morgan Stanley maintained an overweight rating on the stock with a target price of HK$325, citing an estimated 20 billion yuan in cash that could be used to enhance shareholder returns.
Citi also reaffirmed Pop Mart as a top pick, pointing to a global consumer survey suggesting that overseas demand for the company’s non-Labubu intellectual properties may be underestimated.

data.title: As LVMH returns to growth, is the slump in China’s luxury market finally over?
data.description:
French luxury giant LVMH recorded an uptick in sales in China during the last three months of 2025, in a further sign that the vast Chinese luxury market is starting to stabilise after a sharp slowdown the previous year.
The group reported on Tuesday that its total annual sales last year reached €80.8 billion (US$97 billion), down 5 per cent year on year on a reported basis. But performance improved in the second half of the year as sales returned to organic growth, rising 1 per cent.
This was partly driven by positive growth in most of Asia. While Japan saw sales decline, the rest of the region recorded a 1 per cent increase in sales during the fourth quarter.
“Performance among Chinese customers is broadly comparable to the third quarter, with the local market remaining positive,” said Cecile Cabanis, LVMH’s chief financial officer.
Analysts had been eagerly waiting for LVMH’s latest results in China to get a better sense of the luxury market’s trajectory. Sales figures released by other groups earlier this month had presented a mixed picture.
British brand Burberry reported 6 per cent year on year growth in China during the final quarter of 2025, up from 3 per cent the previous quarter. But Swiss group Richemont saw its sales growth in mainland China, Hong Kong and Macau slow to 2 per cent, down from 7 per cent in the third quarter.


LVMH, the world’s biggest luxury group, still faces challenges as operating profits for its core business – fashion and leather goods – declined by 13 per cent in 2025. Margins were under pressure due to growth stagnation, according to Cabanis.
As expected, operating profits for the group’s wine and spirits division also declined sharply – by 25 per cent – mainly due to tariff rises in China and the United States, two of the company’s biggest markets.
The administration of US President Donald Trump slapped a 15 per cent duty on European wine and spirits last year, while China imposed a minimum price on most imports of French cognac after an anti-dumping investigation.
Still, the signs of stabilisation in China are welcome news for the luxury industry, which remains heavily reliant on demand from Chinese consumers.
Companies were hit hard when luxury sales in China declined by up to 20 per cent in 2024, as households cut back on spending amid an economic slowdown and lingering property downturn.
French and European brands had to rethink their China strategies as local customers became more cautious. LVMH was among the firms to double down on the Chinese market, investing in new flagship stores in major cities such as Louis Vuitton’s ship-shaped outlet “The Louis” in Shanghai.
Marc-Antoine Jamet, LVMH’s general secretary, said the group had full confidence in the Chinese market, which he called the “beating heart of global luxury”, in an interview with the Post in November.

data.title: Hong Kong hit with record cyberattacks in 2025 as AI-linked threats rise
data.description:
The number of cyberattacks in Hong Kong hit a record high in 2025, with detected system vulnerabilities tripling, prompting experts to warn that artificial intelligence (AI) could pose a major risk of further attacks this year.
Edmond Lai Shiao-bun, chief digital officer of the Hong Kong Productivity Council, warned on Wednesday that AI would pose three of the top five security threats to enterprises this year, urging firms to invest more in cybersecurity defences and staffing.
“It may not be appropriate for us to let employees ponder this issue. Instead, we need more top-down guidance on how to better use AI. This is an inevitable trend,” Lai said.
The council’s Hong Kong Computer Emergency Response Team Coordination Centre has outlined five major risks for the coming year: AI-driven attacks and the misuse of AI agents; weak corporate governance over AI use; security gaps involving third-party service providers and supply chains; over-reliance on cloud services; and threats posed by AI-powered robots.
Lai revealed that the centre recorded 18,577 cybersecurity incidents and loopholes last year, 57 per cent of which, or 8,973 cases, were phishing attacks.
The total number of incidents and loopholes recorded by the centre last year was a record high.
However, the number of cases involving vulnerable systems rose by 368 per cent from 2024 to 2,328 last year.
Lai said that the surge stemmed from a preventive operation by another cybersecurity firm, which tested companies’ computer systems to identify existing loopholes.
He also said the dominance of phishing attacks was directly linked to the global rise of AI, as fraudulent emails had become far more convincing and numerous.
“We are sure that hackers are exploiting AI to quickly generate large volumes of emails in accordance with the latest trends,” Lai said.
Among the phishing cases recorded, the centre found that the largest share – 34 per cent – of fraudulent links was sent via social media platforms. It was followed by cryptocurrency and banking or e-payment platforms, which accounted for another 18 per cent and 17 per cent, respectively.

The chief digital officer warned that firms’ growing use of AI posed new cybersecurity risks, as such tools might fail to detect fraud while performing their functions, saying that no technological solution yet existed to address these threats.
Agentic AI systems were cited as a particular concern, since they can automatically execute tasks such as replying to emails or opening websites.
“If the email it read contained a phishing link, it would be like an employee with no cybersecurity awareness clicking on it and providing the requested information,” Lai said. “That would essentially mean leaking your email address and password.”
Lai also warned that when employees used publicly available generative AI tools to process internal company information, that data could be incorporated into the models’ training.
“If you provide confidential or client-related data, that would be equivalent to a leak,” he said.
“Therefore, both large and small enterprises should establish clear guidelines for staff using these systems to prevent increasing risk exposure while boosting productivity.”
A survey conducted by the centre last October and November found that 33 per cent of small and medium-sized enterprises had no staff dedicated to cybersecurity, while 5 per cent of large companies had the same issue.
The centre surveyed 544 small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), 78 large companies and 50 cybersecurity service providers.
Only 5 per cent of SMEs said they planned to increase cybersecurity staffing this year, while 13 per cent intended to raise budgets and training in the area.
But 15 per cent of large firms said they would boost manpower, and 38 per cent and 36 per cent planned to enhance training and budgets, respectively.
Lai urged companies to strengthen cybersecurity staffing, issue clear AI-use guidelines, and reinforce information access and security measures.
The chief digital officer added that he believed the critical infrastructure law passed last year, which requires firms in eight designated “critical infrastructure” sectors to conduct annual system checks, would have a “ripple effect” on other companies.
“For companies that are not affected, hopefully this will prompt them to start thinking about it, as clearer guidelines on [information security] become available,” Lai said.
